iran_crude: Iran remains the only Gulf state maintaining near-normal crude loadings, averaging ~2.0 mb/d in 9–15 Mar and 23–29 Mar, with a dip to 1.5 mb/d in 16–22 Mar — broadly in line with pre-crisis run-rates, per Kpler tracking of Kharg Island loadings at ~1.84 mb/d.

China appeared as the sole identified buyer in 23–29 Mar (~148 kbd), consistent with Iran's 'friendly nation' Hormuz transit policy granting passage to Chinese, Russian, Indian, Iraqi, and Pakistani vessels — effectively turning Iran's adversary status into a crude marketing advantage.

The US struck 90+ military targets on Kharg Island on Mar 13 but deliberately spared oil infrastructure, per Bloomberg — Tehran's export lifeline intact for now, though Trump is reportedly weighing seizure of the island, which would remove ~2 mb/d from global supply at a stroke.
iran_naphtha: Iran naphtha exports collapsed to zero after a single 46 kbd cargo to the UAE in 9–15 Mar — consistent with the broader shutdown of intra-Gulf refined product trade as Hormuz transits became untenable for non-Iranian flagged tankers.
iraq_crude: Iraq's seaborne crude exports are functionally dead — Basra output at ~800 kbd vs pre-war 3.3 mb/d — but the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey restarted mid-March and has stabilised at its ~250 kbd capacity (23–29 Mar and 30 Mar–2 Apr), now Iraq's sole crude export route.

The 92% collapse in Iraqi exports (from ~3.3 mb/d to 0.25 mb/d) reflects the Iraq Oil Ministry's Mar 20 FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields. Onshore storage at Basra is filling rapidly, with IEA warning zero Hormuz cargo transits expected in April.

Iraq has reportedly begun routing fuel oil overland through Syria as a stopgap. But with Basra terminals offline and Kirkuk-Ceyhan maxed at 250 kbd, the country has effectively lost 93% of its export capacity — the single largest national supply disruption in the crisis.
iraq_naphtha: Iraq naphtha exports are negligible and sporadic (~13–20 kbd), with a small cargo to the UAE appearing in 30 Mar–2 Apr — likely residual volumes from limited Kurdish region processing rather than southern operations, which are shut under the FM declaration.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait crude exports have been effectively zeroed out since the conflict began — a single anomalous 78 kbd reading in 16–22 Mar aside, no loadings have occurred across all four weeks tracked.

KPC's FM declaration, compounded by IRGC drone strikes on Al-Ahmadi refinery (Mar 19-20) and Kuwait airport fuel tanks (Apr 1), has eliminated Kuwait's entire ~2.6 mb/d export baseline. With no pipeline bypass route and 3–4 months estimated for post-war restoration, Kuwait faces the longest recovery timeline of any Gulf exporter.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Kuwait diesel exports collapsed from 102 kbd in 9–15 Mar (with Qatar as sole buyer at 48 kbd) to zero for the following three weeks — consistent with Al-Ahmadi refinery going offline after the Mar 19-20 drone strikes and Bapco FM halting regional product flows.
kuwait_gasoline: A single 64 kbd gasoline cargo went to Saudi Arabia in 9–15 Mar before Kuwait's refined product exports ceased entirely — Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction and KPC's blanket FM have eliminated all downstream export capability.
oman_crude: Oman has emerged as the crisis's biggest crude export winner, doubling output from 550 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) — driven entirely by its geographic position outside the Hormuz chokepoint and inland production base (~950 kbd intact).

China is absorbing virtually all Omani crude, with Taiwan appearing briefly as a second buyer in 16–22 Mar (269 kbd). Oman's benchmark has surged to $152–153/bbl for May delivery, reflecting extreme Asian premium for non-Hormuz barrels.

Three Omani-flagged vessels (2 VLCCs + 1 LNG carrier) transited Hormuz via an alternative coastal route on Apr 2, with an Iran-Oman transit protocol being drafted — potentially reopening a narrow corridor that could further boost Oman's role as a Gulf-to-Asia bridge.
oman_lng: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility — critically positioned outside Hormuz — is running at capacity and commanding significant Asian spot premiums with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline. Exports averaged 30–43 ktons/day across March, diversified across India, Japan, Thailand, China, and Taiwan.

India has become the dominant buyer, rising from zero in 9–15 Mar to sole recipient in 30 Mar–2 Apr (17.2 ktons/day), consistent with Petronet LNG's FM on Qatar contracts and India's scramble for alternative LNG supply.
Japan and China disappeared from the buyer list after 16–22 Mar — likely securing US spot and Australian contracts instead as JKM prices hit $20.39/MMBtu, 2x pre-crisis forecasts.
oman_kero_jet: Oman jet fuel exports appeared only in 16–22 Mar (25 kbd) and 23–29 Mar (46 kbd), both going exclusively to Djibouti — likely supplying the major military base complex (US, French, Chinese) that has become a logistics hub for the Hormuz crisis response.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman diesel exports surged 4.6x from 50 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 229 kbd (23–29 Mar), filling the vacuum left by Kuwait and UAE refinery shutdowns — with buyer base expanding from Pakistan alone to five countries including South Africa, Mozambique, Mauritius, and Singapore.

The rapid diversification into African and Southeast Asian markets reflects Oman's Duqm refinery leveraging its Indian Ocean access to serve customers cut off from traditional Gulf diesel supply chains. Pakistan remains the anchor buyer at ~50 kbd throughout.
oman_gasoline: Oman gasoline exports nearly tripled from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 120 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Saudi Arabia appearing as a buyer at 47 kbd — a remarkable reversal where the world's largest oil exporter is importing gasoline from its smaller neighbour.

African markets (Tanzania, Sudan, South Africa) and Pakistan are the primary beneficiaries of Oman's expanded refinery output. The surge reflects Oman's Duqm and Sohar refineries stepping into the gap left by BAPCO (Bahrain, 405 kbd struck) and Al-Ahmadi (Kuwait, offline).
oman_naphtha: Oman naphtha exports dried up after two weeks — South Korea took 72 kbd in 9–15 Mar, India 29 kbd in 16–22 Mar, then zero. Feedstock-starved Asian crackers (FPCC at ~50% capacity, YNCC under FM) may be outbid by crude buyers or switching to alternative feedstocks.
qatar_crude: Qatar crude exports are effectively zero — all offshore fields (~440 kbd) shut since early March under QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM declaration. A single 117 kbd reading in 16–22 Mar (to UAE) appears to be a final cargo from pre-crisis storage rather than new production.

With Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 offline and the entire Ras Laffan/Mesaieed complex shut, Qatar has no path to resuming crude flows until the conflict ends. The Mar 18 Iranian strike on Ras Laffan caused damage that QatarEnergy estimates will take 3–5 years to repair.
qatar_lng: Qatar LNG exports have collapsed from ~211 ktons/day (pre-crisis, ~77 Mtpa annualised) to a trickle of 6–18 ktons/day — a 92% reduction representing the single largest commodity supply disruption in the crisis.

The tiny residual volumes (totalling ~243 ktons over 4 weeks) are going exclusively to Kuwait and Pakistan via short intra-Gulf routes, likely from pre-loaded storage. QatarEnergy extended FM through mid-June to global buyers including Italy, Belgium, South Korea, and China (Mar 24), with Edison notified of FM on 10 cargoes.

Iran's Mar 18 strike on Ras Laffan damaged 12.8 Mtpa of capacity with a 3–5 year repair timeline. Even if Hormuz reopens, Qatar can only restart ~64 Mtpa of its 77 Mtpa nameplate — a structural LNG deficit that will persist well beyond any ceasefire.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports recovered to ~6.2 mb/d (23–29 Mar) via the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu, up from 3.9 mb/d in the first post-crisis week — approaching the pipeline's maximum 7 mb/d throughput reported by Fortune. Aramco is slashing Asia supply allocations for a second consecutive month.

Egypt has emerged as the largest crude destination (1.0–2.0 mb/d across all weeks), reflecting the Yanbu-SUMED pipeline routing Saudi barrels to the Mediterranean — effectively re-exporting to European refiners who lost direct Gulf access.

China and India remain the 2nd and 3rd largest buyers (~1.0–1.7 mb/d each), but Myanmar (572 kbd, 23–29 Mar) and Malaysia appeared as new buyers — signalling Southeast Asian refiners are scrambling for non-Hormuz supply as traditional Gulf flows evaporate.

The pipeline bypass is working but capped — with Yanbu at maximum throughput, Saudi exports are structurally limited to ~5–6 mb/d versus pre-war ~7 mb/d through Hormuz, and the Red Sea route faces its own Bab el-Mandeb security risks.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel exports are volatile — 46 kbd (9–15 Mar), spiking to 95 kbd to the UK (16–22 Mar), dropping to zero (23–29 Mar), then surging to 172 kbd (30 Mar–2 Apr) — reflecting lumpy Yanbu loadings and prioritisation of crude over products in the constrained pipeline.

The UK appearing as the sole identified buyer in 16–22 Mar likely reflects a strategic military supply arrangement routing via the Red Sea, given the massive NATO/coalition naval presence in the region.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports are averaging ~500–650 kbd via Yanbu, serving an unusually diversified buyer base spanning three continents: Egypt and Kenya in Africa, Italy/Netherlands/Belgium/Spain/Slovenia in Europe, and Malaysia/Pakistan in Asia.

The shift from Asia-dominated pre-war diesel flows to a pan-European/African customer mix reflects Yanbu's Red Sea geography — Mediterranean buyers who previously had no Saudi diesel supply chain are now the path of least resistance, while East Asian buyers are cut off.

Kenya's emergence as a top buyer (107–153 kbd) alongside traditional Middle East clients signals East Africa's acute diesel vulnerability — likely replacing UAE and Bahrain-sourced barrels lost to refinery shutdowns and FM declarations.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports peaked at 374 kbd (16–22 Mar) via Yanbu before declining to 229 kbd (23–29 Mar) and zero in early April — suggesting Aramco is prioritising crude and diesel in its constrained pipeline allocation.

Singapore emerged as the anchor buyer (42–138 kbd), likely reflecting its role as Asia's gasoline trading hub. Malaysia (91 kbd in 16–22 Mar) and East African markets (Kenya, Sudan) are filling via the Red Sea route.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports are steady at 73–133 kbd via Yanbu, with Indonesia and Japan alternating as buyers — a lifeline for Asian naphtha crackers like FPCC (Taiwan, ~50% capacity) and YNCC (South Korea, under FM) that have lost Gulf feedstock from every other supplier.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports via ADCOP (Fujairah) surged to 3.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) — a record per S&P Platts — before collapsing to just 242 kbd (30 Mar–2 Apr), reflecting the extreme loading lumpiness of Fujairah's single pipeline with limited storage.

The buyer mix is Asia's most diversified: India (700 kbd), South Korea (514 kbd), Japan (257 kbd), Vietnam (257 kbd), and Philippines (139 kbd) all appeared in 23–29 Mar, as ADNOC prices crude under a new Inside/Outside Strait bifurcation structure launched Apr 1.

Fujairah loadings averaged ~1.6 mb/d over the four-week period — roughly 57% of UAE's pre-war ~2.8 mb/d total exports, per World Oil. With Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Shah Gas still shut, ADCOP crude is essentially the UAE's only functioning export channel.
uae_gasoil_diesel: UAE diesel exports collapsed to zero after a single 73 kbd cargo to Pakistan in 9–15 Mar — Ruwais Refinery-2 going offline eliminated the UAE's main diesel export source, and the Fujairah bunkering hub (world's 2nd largest) remains paralysed under MEE and Pearl Marine FM declarations.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports emerged from zero in 23–29 Mar with a 46 kbd cargo to Taiwan, rising to 80 kbd in 30 Mar–2 Apr — likely sourced from Fujairah storage or Habshan gas complex (which resumed Mar 23 after a 4-day emergency shutdown), providing a small relief to naphtha-starved Asian crackers.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude re-exports are marginal (~0.05-0.20 mb/d) and concentrated on intra-European destinations (UK, Finland) with zero Gulf-origin barrels in any week.
Volumes dropped to zero in 30 Mar-2 Apr, consistent with the European Commission's Mar 31 directive urging member states to prioritize domestic supply and defer non-emergency refinery maintenance.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU jet fuel exports collapsed 77% to just 34 kbd/d (23-29 Mar) from 146 kbd/d the prior fortnight, as European refiners retained cargoes domestically amid acute ME product supply loss -- IEA warned diesel and jet markets are 'particularly vulnerable' to extended Hormuz disruption.

Exports rebounded to ~130 kbd/d in 30 Mar-2 Apr with UK taking 72 kbd/d, suggesting the drawdown was a brief inventory-building pulse rather than a structural shift; watch for further volatility as the EU Commission weighs export restrictions.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU diesel exports fell 36% to ~760 kbd/d (23-29 Mar) from ~1.19 mb/d the prior week, directly following the European Commission's Mar 31 call to preserve domestic product availability -- OPIS reports the crisis has 'left Europe in the hands of US diesel producers.'

Destination mix diversified toward Mediterranean and West Africa (Croatia 96 kbd/d, Italy 84 kbd/d, Mauritania 49 kbd/d), suggesting EU diesel is partly replacing lost Gulf supplies in regional markets that previously drew from ME refineries.

With Middle East exports contributing ~23M mt of EU diesel imports in 2024 now effectively offline, the simultaneous contraction in EU diesel exports signals refiners are pivoting to backfill domestic shortfalls rather than re-exporting.
eu_27_gasoline: EU gasoline exports held steady at ~1.1-1.4 mb/d through March, bucking the diesel contraction -- Africa emerged as the key growth market with Egypt surging to 151 kbd/d (23-29 Mar), Kenya steady at ~111 kbd/d, and Senegal appearing at 73 kbd/d as ME-sourced product vanished.

Saudi Arabia imported EU gasoline (37 kbd/d) in 9-15 Mar -- a striking reversal for the world's largest crude exporter, reflecting the severity of Gulf refinery disruptions including BAPCO Sitra destruction (405 kbd/d offline) and Kuwait Al-Ahmadi drone strikes.

Partial-week data (30 Mar-2 Apr) shows exports dropping to ~853 kbd/d with cargoes redirecting to southern Africa (Namibia 176 kbd/d, South Africa 166 kbd/d), filling a vacuum as ME refined product supply evaporates further in April.
eu_27_naphtha: EU naphtha exports held at ~350-410 kbd/d but the buyer mix shifted dramatically: Japan surged from zero to 103 kbd/d (23-29 Mar) and China appeared at 49 kbd/d, as both scramble for non-Gulf feedstock after South Korea's Mar 27 naphtha export ban and FPCC's 50% cracker rate cuts in Taiwan.

South Korea took 73 kbd/d in 9-15 Mar but disappeared entirely by late March -- consistent with Seoul's export ban to preserve domestic supply -- while intra-European flows (Spain, Belgium, Italy) provide baseline demand for refinery integration.
russia_crude: Russian crude exports declined 20% from ~6.7 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~5.4 mb/d (23-29 Mar), with China still dominant at 1.3 mb/d but India falling sharply to 803 kbd/d from 1.6 mb/d -- partly reflecting India's 'friendly nation' Hormuz transit deal with Iran reducing urgency for Russian alternatives.

Turkey emerged as a major incremental buyer, surging to 666 kbd/d (23-29 Mar) from 116 kbd/d, likely acting as a refining and re-export hub; Italy and France remain consistent at 300-660 kbd/d each via shadow fleet (56% of Russian crude now shipped on sanctioned vessels per CREA).

Israel imported Russian crude (105 kbd/d, 9-15 Mar) while Philippines appeared at 212 kbd/d (16-22 Mar) consistent with Petron's 2.48M bbl deal -- the crisis is generating previously unthinkable trade flows.

With the EU Russian LNG ban (Apr 25), Russia's own gasoline export ban (Apr 1), and OPEC+ meeting Apr 5, expect further concentration of Russian crude flows on fewer Asian buyers at steepening discounts.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports averaged 80-111 ktons/d, with EU nations (France, Belgium, Spain, Netherlands) collectively taking ~50-70% of volumes -- a race to lock in cargoes before the EU's Apr 25 ban on Russian LNG purchases takes effect.

China's share was volatile (9-28 ktons/d across weeks), and Asian buyers showed less urgency for Russian LNG compared to crude, partly because US LNG is filling the Qatar gap with Cheniere running at 110% nameplate utilization.

Post-Apr 25 expect a sharp reorientation toward China, India, and Turkey as EU access closes; Arctic LNG-2 remains below capacity, capping Russia's ability to fully benefit from the ~39% surge in Asian spot LNG prices.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports plunged 64% from ~1.0 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~360 kbd/d (23-29 Mar), with Turkey absorbing the majority at 211 kbd/d and Brazil the only other consistent buyer -- Syria received 64 kbd/d in early March but disappeared as logistics unraveled.

The collapse reflects both reduced Russian refinery runs (Moscow's gasoline export ban Apr 1 signals domestic fuel prioritization) and loss of the Egypt/Ghana/Sudan customer base that appeared in earlier weeks but subsequently dropped out.
russia_gasoline: Russia's gasoline exports were negligible -- a single 37.5 kbd/d cargo to Libya in 9-15 Mar, followed by three consecutive weeks of zero exports, formalizing Russia's Apr 1 gasoline export ban that prioritizes domestic supply amid surging pump prices.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports declined 37% from ~677 kbd/d to ~428 kbd/d over March, with Saudi Arabia importing 71 kbd/d (9-15 Mar) -- a remarkable reversal as the kingdom sources Russian feedstock for petrochemical crackers while its own NGL output is curtailed by offshore field shutdowns.

Indonesia appeared as a new buyer at 115 kbd/d (16-22 Mar), consistent with Chandra Asri's FM declaration citing Middle East feedstock loss; partial-week W14 data (82 kbd/d, Morocco only) suggests a sharp near-term drop as Russia diverts more feed to domestic refineries.
russia_sulphur: Russian sulphur exports are negligible (0.4-19 ktons/d) and almost entirely to Morocco for OCP Group's phosphate fertilizer production; China appeared briefly at 11 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) as Gulf sulphur supplies from Qatar and UAE smelters ceased entirely following FM declarations.
us_crude: US crude exports surged 15% to ~4.5 mb/d (23-29 Mar), near all-time records, as the world scrambles for non-Gulf barrels -- Netherlands (716 kbd/d), South Korea (509 kbd/d), and Japan (480 kbd/d) were the largest takers with Brent at $108-112.

China appeared as a US crude buyer at 224 kbd/d (23-29 Mar) -- a significant geopolitical signal given ongoing trade tensions -- while India took 249 kbd/d, supplementing its naval-escorted Gulf crude convoys under Operation Urja Suraksha.

The buyer base expanded to 15 countries in 23-29 Mar including first-time destinations Denmark (205 kbd/d), Spain (410 kbd/d), and Australia (86 kbd/d), signaling structural substitution of Gulf barrels rather than temporary spot trades.

With IEA warning April losses could be 'twice March' and OPEC+ set to meet Apr 5, US Gulf Coast exporters are positioned as the marginal swing supplier -- but pipeline-to-port logistics cap near-term upside at approximately 4.5-5.0 mb/d.
us_lng: US LNG exports held steady at ~350-376 ktons/d, reflecting all 8 terminals running at maximum capacity -- Sabine Pass at 110% nameplate -- as the US becomes the world's largest LNG exporter with Qatar's 77 Mtpa fully offline.

Destination mix rotating toward Asia: Thailand appeared at 34 ktons/d (23-29 Mar), Bangladesh at 9-10 ktons/d, and India at 18-20 ktons/d, filling the gap left by QatarEnergy's FM which supplied 41% of India's LNG.

Europe remains a major destination (Netherlands 42-72 ktons/d, France 31-73 ktons/d, Germany 10-41 ktons/d) as the EU scrambles to replace both Qatar LNG and impending Apr 25 Russian LNG ban -- Golden Pass LNG start-up adds incremental capacity.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rose 11% from ~148 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to ~164 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL supply collapsed -- Argus confirms the LPG-naphtha spread now strongly favors US Gulf Coast cargoes.

Japan is the anchor buyer at 28-33 ktons/d, but new Asian destinations are emerging: Indonesia rose to 14 ktons/d, China surged to 22 ktons/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), and Thailand appeared at 11 ktons/d, consistent with China's PDH plant operators scrambling for feedstock per ICIS.
Watch for further acceleration as Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals ramp to serve the structural gap -- Gulf NGL producers estimate 3-4 months to restore capacity post-conflict per KPC.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports rose 47% from ~214 kbd/d (9-15 Mar) to ~314 kbd/d (23-29 Mar), with the UK emerging as the dominant buyer at 134 kbd/d -- reflecting Europe's acute jet fuel vulnerability as ME refineries go offline and the EU Commission urged member states to defer refinery maintenance.

Netherlands consistently took 44-76 kbd/d as the ARA hub channels US jet imports into continental Europe; Latin American buyers (Mexico, Brazil, Peru) provide baseline demand but are being displaced by higher-margin European flows.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports surged 51% to ~1.82 mb/d (23-29 Mar) -- the highest level in the four-week window -- as Netherlands jumped to 349 kbd/d and Peru to 173 kbd/d, confirming OPIS's assessment that the 'Middle East crisis leaves Europe in the hands of US diesel producers.'

Geographic reach expanded dramatically: Australia appeared at 87 kbd/d, Morocco at 43 kbd/d, and Canada at 40 kbd/d, while traditional Caribbean/Latin American buyers (Jamaica 54 kbd/d, Guatemala 43 kbd/d, Trinidad 41 kbd/d) maintained steady draws.

With EU diesel exports contracting 36% the same week and IEA flagging diesel/jet as 'particularly vulnerable,' US Gulf Coast refineries face their strongest margin environment in years -- but at the cost of $4+/gallon domestic gasoline prices.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports remained stable at ~810-830 kbd/d through March, primarily serving the Americas -- Mexico alone took 158-311 kbd/d -- but notable new buyers appeared: Japan (46 kbd/d, 23-29 Mar) and Australia (87 kbd/d, 16-22 Mar), reflecting Asian fuel shortages reaching non-traditional markets.

The stability masks growing domestic tension: with US gas past $4/gal and Brent at $108-112, DOE may face pressure to curtail product exports if prices continue rising -- the same political dynamic that drove Russia's Apr 1 gasoline export ban.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports spiked 81% to ~759 kbd/d (16-22 Mar), driven by South Korea taking 251 kbd/d as ME naphtha vanished -- YNCC declared FM on ethylene production due to 'total Middle East naphtha supply suspension,' and Seoul banned naphtha exports Mar 27.

Japan is a consistent buyer at 76-122 kbd/d, while Venezuela (113-150 kbd/d) and Colombia (92 kbd/d) rely on US naphtha as refinery diluent; UK appeared at 75 kbd/d in 30 Mar-2 Apr, an unusual trans-Atlantic flow likely supplying European petrochem crackers facing LyondellBasell's FM on polyolefins.

With naphtha surging past $1,000/mt per Formosa Plastics and Asian crackers running at 50% rates, US Gulf Coast naphtha is commanding structural premiums that may persist well beyond the Hormuz closure.
australia_crude: Australian crude exports are small (~180-280 kbd/d) but strategically vital: South Korea is the anchor buyer at 93-146 kbd/d, while Japan appeared at 162 kbd/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), likely diversifying its 94.2% ME crude dependence as onshore inventories fell 9% below seasonal average per Reuters.

With Australia and Singapore agreeing to maintain bilateral energy flows, Australian condensate and light crude are commanding significant premiums as non-Hormuz alternatives for Asia-Pacific refiners.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports declined from ~252 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to ~169 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) -- likely reflecting production already at maximum capacity with no room to ramp, rather than demand weakness, given the massive Qatar supply gap.

Japan remains the anchor at 48-71 ktons/d, but Thailand emerged as a consistent new buyer (11-20 ktons/d across all four weeks), consistent with Thailand's growing LNG import dependency as domestic gas from Gulf of Thailand depletes.

Australia's ~90 Mtpa LNG capacity (~246 ktons/d) is the single largest source of non-disrupted LNG for Asia -- with Qatar's 77 Mtpa fully offline and US terminals maxed out, any Australian supply interruption would be catastrophic for regional energy security.
australia_naphtha: Australian naphtha exports are sporadic and minimal (~49 kbd/d in two of four weeks, entirely to Singapore), reflecting Australia's position as a net crude/condensate exporter rather than a refined products producer -- Asian naphtha demand must be met by US and EU suppliers.
china_crude: China's crude imports plunged 38% from ~11.2 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~7.0 mb/d (30 Mar-2 Apr) as the Hormuz closure systematically eliminated Gulf suppliers -- Saudi Arabia, Iraq, UAE, and Kuwait all vanished from the supplier list by late March, collapsing Gulf share from 44% to 19%.

Russia remains the anchor at ~2.0-2.3 mb/d throughout, but cannot fill a ~4 mb/d Gulf gap alone; Brazil (1.4 mb/d), Angola (1.3 mb/d), and Oman (0.5 mb/d) have emerged as critical swing suppliers, while pipeline-locked volumes (ESPO ~600 kbd, Kazakhstan ~220 kbd, Myanmar ~200 kbd) provide ~1.0 mb/d of Hormuz-independent floor.

Beijing's Jan-Feb strategic stockpiling (11.99 mb/d, +16% YoY per Bloomberg) provided a buffer, but the March drawdown trajectory suggests inventories are now depleting rapidly -- the fuel export ban since Mar 4-5 and Shell-CNOOC Huizhou cracker shutdown signal domestic rationing has begun.

With OPEC+ meeting Apr 5 and the Apr 6 deadline looming, China faces a structural supply deficit: even if Hormuz partially reopens, rebuilding Gulf supply chains to pre-crisis levels will take weeks as 3,200 vessels remain trapped in the Gulf (4% of global tonnage).
china_lng: Qatar LNG -- previously China's top Gulf gas supplier at 45 ktons/d (34% share in 9-15 Mar) -- dropped to zero by 23-29 Mar following QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24, removing ~20% of global LNG supply from the market.

Australia surged to fill the gap, peaking at 52.4 ktons/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), with Malaysia, PNG, and Russia providing supplementary volumes; Oman LNG (outside Hormuz) re-emerged at 19.2 ktons/d in early April, offering the only Gulf-proximate alternative.

Total imports fell 39% from 133.8 to 81.7 ktons/d in 23-29 Mar before partially recovering to 116.2 ktons/d -- Power of Siberia pipeline gas provides partial insulation, but spot LNG costs have surged ~39%, squeezing margins for Chinese PDH plants and city gas distributors.
china_lpg: US Gulf Coast terminals (Enterprise, Targa) have become China's dominant LPG supplier at 24-30 ktons/d, replacing Saudi/Qatar/UAE NGL-linked volumes that collapsed alongside upstream shutdowns; Iran continues trickling LPG through IRGC-tolled Hormuz passage at 5-15 ktons/d.

Total LPG imports declined 35% from 59.1 to 38.3 ktons/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), tightening feedstock for China's world-leading PDH plant fleet; the LPG-naphtha spread has shifted as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked constraints, per ICIS.
china_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports collapsed from 24 kbd (9-15 Mar, entirely South Korea-sourced) to zero by 16-22 Mar and remained at zero through early April -- consistent with China's fuel export ban and South Korea's own product conservation measures amid cratering crude supply.
china_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports, already minimal at 10-15 kbd from regional refiners (South Korea, Japan), fell to zero by 30 Mar-2 Apr; China's domestic refining capacity is sufficient when crude feedstock is available, but the sharp crude import decline suggests product tightness is imminent.
china_naphtha: Gulf naphtha suppliers were systematically eliminated: Kuwait (124 kbd in 9-15 Mar) vanished after Al-Ahmadi drone strikes, Qatar and UAE followed, and Gulf share crashed from 46% to zero by 23-29 Mar -- directly triggering Shell-CNOOC's Huizhou ethylene cracker shutdown and Wanhua Chemical's FM declaration.

Russia stepped in as the primary replacement at 89-106 kbd, joined by non-traditional sources including Norway (77 kbd), Thailand (48 kbd), India (136 kbd in 30 Mar-2 Apr), and South Korea -- but total imports still fell 45% from 409 to 225 kbd, well below China's ~600 kbd petrochemical demand, pointing to sustained cracker curtailments.
india_crude: India's crude imports surged to ~6.1 mb/d (30 Mar-2 Apr) from a crisis low of ~3.6 mb/d (9-15 Mar), driven by Russia exploding to 3.3 mb/d (55% share) -- a direct result of India-Russia crude supply talks restarted Mar 19 and Operation Urja Suraksha naval escorts through Hormuz.

Gulf suppliers re-emerged intermittently: Saudi at 0.78-0.95 mb/d and UAE at 0.45-0.49 mb/d (likely cargoes loaded via Yanbu/ADCOP bypass pipelines), while new sources -- Brazil (0.68 mb/d), Nigeria (0.41 mb/d), Ecuador (0.30 mb/d) -- show aggressive multi-source diversification.

Iran's 'friendly nation' safe-passage status for India is enabling Hormuz transits under naval escort, but India's crude trade deficit is still set to widen by >$4B in March per Business Today; MRPL's 500 kbd refinery throughput cuts and Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cut underscore that domestic supply remains severely strained despite rising imports.
india_lng: Qatar -- formerly 41% of India's LNG supply -- is completely absent, consistent with Petronet LNG's FM declaration; Oman (outside Hormuz) emerged as the top Gulf replacement at 32.1 ktons/d in 23-29 Mar, while Nigeria (17-21 ktons/d) and Angola (10-34 ktons/d) became critical African swing sources.

Total LNG imports rebounded sharply from 31.2 ktons/d (16-22 Mar) to 78.6 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) -- more than doubling -- as US spot cargoes (19.3 ktons/d) and diversified African supply partially compensated for the Qatar gap; India-Russia LNG talks following the Puri-Sorokin meeting Mar 19 may unlock additional volumes.
india_lpg: Indian Navy-escorted LPG convoys (Pine Gas and Jag Vasant, 92,000t combined) under Operation Urja Suraksha produced a dramatic result: LPG imports nearly tripled from 14.6 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) to 33.9 ktons/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), with Gulf share surging from 29% to 70% as Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi, UAE, and even Iran volumes resumed.

Iran's re-appearance as a supplier at 6.8 ktons/d confirms India's 'friendly nation' safe-passage is enabling flows that other Asian importers cannot access; the US remains the baseline non-Gulf supplier at ~8.6-10.4 ktons/d, providing structural diversification.
india_naphtha: India's naphtha imports more than doubled from 101 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 257 kbd (30 Mar-2 Apr), with Russia surging from 53 to 206 kbd -- India appears to be absorbing Russian naphtha barrels displaced from European markets, creating a new structural trade flow.

Oman is the only Gulf naphtha supplier at 46-51 kbd (benefiting from its position outside Hormuz), while UAE naphtha that appeared in 9-15 Mar (48 kbd) has not returned; rising Indian naphtha imports are likely feeding both domestic petrochemical demand and re-exports to naphtha-starved Asian crackers.
japan_crude: Japan's crude imports collapsed 63% from ~2.0 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~0.75 mb/d (30 Mar-2 Apr) -- the sharpest decline among major Asian importers -- with Gulf share remaining at 95-100% throughout because Japan has no alternative supplier relationships (unlike China/India which pivoted to Russia).

Saudi Arabia (0.50 mb/d) and UAE (0.13 mb/d) remain the only suppliers, delivering via Yanbu East-West Pipeline and ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah respectively; the 80 mb SPR release (Japan's largest ever, per Reuters) is the sole buffer preventing refinery shutdowns, and METI has warned additional releases may be needed past April.

Japan's structural vulnerability is now fully exposed: 94.2% ME crude dependence with zero Russian or Atlantic Basin supply alternatives means every barrel must transit Hormuz or bypass pipelines operating at maximum capacity -- a supply chain with zero resilience if the Apr 6 deadline passes without resolution.
japan_lng: Japan LNG imports halved from 201 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to 90.1 ktons/d (30 Mar-2 Apr); Australia -- the backstop supplier -- swung wildly from 80.8 to 111.1 back down to 17.9 ktons/d, suggesting Australian export capacity is being competed away by other Asian buyers.

Qatar LNG dropped to zero by 23-29 Mar (from 10.7 ktons/d), consistent with Shell and TotalEnergies FM declarations on Qatar-sourced offtake; Japan is now scrambling across 8+ suppliers (Australia, Canada, Malaysia, PNG, Indonesia, Brunei, Mozambique, US) but total volumes are inadequate.

With JKM spot at $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis forecast per CME Group), and Daiwa Institute warning that a 10% ME LNG import cut would push Japan GDP negative, the LNG supply chain is the country's most acute near-term economic risk.
japan_lpg: Japan's LPG imports are 100% US-sourced throughout the crisis, rising from 26.6 to 48.5 ktons/d (9 Mar-2 Apr) -- US Gulf Coast terminals have effectively replaced all former Middle East LPG supply, representing the cleanest and most resilient energy supply chain in Japan's import portfolio.
The near-doubling of US LPG volumes suggests Japanese buyers moved rapidly on long-haul US cargoes, likely at significant freight premiums given Cape of Good Hope rerouting by major container lines.
japan_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports from South Korea held steady at 31-87 kbd throughout the crisis, actually increasing from 30.8 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 86.5 kbd (30 Mar-2 Apr) -- South Korean refineries are maintaining product runs and prioritizing Japan supply even as their own crude imports decline.

This intra-Asian refined product trade is a critical lifeline for Japanese aviation and military operations; any disruption to South Korean refinery operations would immediately cascade to Japanese transport fuel supply.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports remain small and regional (7-53 kbd) sourced from South Korea and intermittently Brunei (49.6 kbd in 16-22 Mar); the declining trend from 52.7 to 7.2 kbd reflects tightening of regional product availability as crude feedstock dries up across Northeast Asian refineries.
japan_gasoline: Gasoline imports tripled from 27.2 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 85.4 kbd (23-29 Mar) as domestic refinery throughput declines forced Japan to increase finished product purchases; India emerged as a new gasoline supplier at 49.5 kbd in 30 Mar-2 Apr, reflecting MRPL/Indian refineries redirecting product from export markets.

The shift from crude-processing to finished-product imports is a structural indicator that Japan's refining sector is rationing crude feedstock -- a costly adaptation given the premium on finished products versus crude in the current market.
japan_naphtha: Naphtha imports swung violently: from 289 kbd (9-15 Mar) to just 86 kbd (16-22 Mar) when only Qatar remained, then recovered to 200 kbd (23-29 Mar) as UAE naphtha via ADCOP (126 kbd) filled the gap -- Kuwait's disappearance (from 70 kbd) aligns with Al-Ahmadi refinery strikes Mar 19-20.

US naphtha emerged at 75 kbd in 30 Mar-2 Apr alongside UAE (77 kbd) and South Korea (42 kbd); Japanese petrochemical producers have been forced to curb output (per TankTerminals), with the naphtha shortage cascading through ethylene, propylene, and polyolefin chains.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports fell 54% from ~3.2 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~1.5 mb/d (30 Mar-2 Apr) as Gulf suppliers collapsed: Saudi went from 1.15 to near-zero, Iraq from 0.55 to zero, Kuwait from 0.26 to zero; Gulf share fell from 76% to 31%.

The US surged to become South Korea's top crude supplier at 0.75 mb/d (30 Mar-2 Apr), up from 0.68 mb/d -- a complete structural reversal from Korea's traditional Gulf-heavy slate; Mexico (0.26 mb/d) appeared as a new source, and UAE (0.45 mb/d via ADCOP) is the sole remaining Gulf lifeline.

Lotte Yeosu halted operations (restart May 29), LG Chem shut Yeosu No. 2, and Korea's naphtha export ban since Mar 27 signal deep refining-sector distress; the SPR swap (2 mb initial, 20+ mb queued per Seoul Economic Daily) is buying time, but inventories are 13% above seasonal norms only because demand destruction is outpacing drawdowns.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG imports fell 44% from 200 ktons/d to 112 ktons/d, but the country is better positioned than Japan: Qatar share was only ~14% of 2026 imports, and KOGAS confirms mandatory reserves are exceeded; new long-term contracts with Woodside (0.5 Mtpa) and TotalEnergies (1 Mtpa) add further insulation.

Australia (34-68 ktons/d) and Malaysia (20-51 ktons/d) are the twin pillars of supply, while Gulf LNG fell to zero by 23-29 Mar; Russia re-emerged at 15.9 ktons/d in 30 Mar-2 Apr as EU's April 25 Russian LNG ban paradoxically redirects cargoes toward Asian buyers.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea's LPG imports are 100% North American (US + Canada), with zero Gulf exposure throughout the crisis; volumes fell from 17.8 to 9.4 ktons/d through March before spiking to 28.1 ktons/d in early April, suggesting a large US cargo arrival after the LPG-naphtha spread shifted to favor increased imports.

The complete absence of Middle Eastern LPG -- historically a significant feedstock for Korean PDH crackers -- is contributing to YNCC's FM declaration on ethylene production due to total ME naphtha/LPG supply suspension.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: Diesel imports are minimal and volatile (0-65 kbd), sourced regionally from Japan, China, and Russia; volumes collapsed to zero by 30 Mar-2 Apr, consistent with regional refineries conserving product amid declining crude runs and tightening supply across Northeast Asia.
south_korea_gasoline: Gasoline imports collapsed from 108 kbd (9-15 Mar, Japan-sourced) to zero by 23-29 Mar and remained at zero -- reflecting either sufficient domestic refinery output for the shrinking demand base or an inability to source affordable cargoes in a market where finished product premiums have surged alongside crude prices at $108-112/bbl.
south_korea_naphtha: The most dramatic supply chain restructuring in the batch: Gulf naphtha went from 96% (501 kbd from UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Saudi in 9-15 Mar) to 0% by 30 Mar-2 Apr, replaced by a wildly diversified mix of India (86 kbd), US (82 kbd), Algeria (75 kbd), Russia (59 kbd), and Greece (52 kbd).

Total volumes fell 32% from 522 to 353 kbd despite the diversification, directly triggering YNCC's FM on ethylene production; South Korea's Mar 27 naphtha export ban is conserving domestic supply but the structural shift from Gulf to Mediterranean/Atlantic Basin naphtha adds 15-25 days of shipping transit time, creating persistent inventory vulnerability.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports contracted 35% from ~1,250 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~815 kbd (23-29 Mar) as Gulf supply chains fractured, before plunging to ~251 kbd in the partial week of 30 Mar-2 Apr as cargoes thinned further.

UAE has become the dominant supplier at 88% share (23-29 Mar), with the US, Brazil, Libya, and Saudi Arabia all disappearing from the supplier mix since mid-March -- a dangerous concentration given Hormuz risk to UAE-origin cargoes routed through Fujairah.

Bangkok secured a bilateral Hormuz transit deal with Iran on Mar 28, joining India and Malaysia in 'friendly nation' passage -- but the deal's practical value remains unproven, and Nigeria/Indonesia emerged as new suppliers in late March signaling active diversification away from Gulf dependence.

With ~90-95 days of reserves per the Energy Ministry and ~60% pre-crisis ME crude dependence, Thailand faces a tightening window if Hormuz remains closed past April -- especially as PTT scrambles to lock in US and West African alternatives per S&P Global.
thailand_lng: Thailand's LNG imports held relatively steady at 25-45 ktons/d through March, with zero Gulf-origin cargoes in most weeks -- reflecting the country's pre-existing diversification toward Australia, which has anchored supply at 10-20 ktons/d throughout the crisis.

The supplier base broadened remarkably: Mozambique, Canada, Malaysia, Oman, China, and the US all delivered cargoes across March, a textbook crisis-driven diversification that insulates Thailand better than most Asian importers dependent on Qatar.

Oman LNG appeared for the first time in 23-29 Mar (~10 ktons/d), exploiting its non-Hormuz export position to capture the Asian spot premium with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline -- expect Oman volumes to grow if Hormuz closure persists.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand's diesel imports are small but volatile, spiking from ~8 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~46 kbd (16-22 Mar) on a single Indian cargo, then falling back to ~8 kbd as Malaysia resumed as the steady supplier.

Zero Gulf-origin diesel across all four weeks confirms Thailand's product supply chain is regionally sourced (Malaysia, India, South Korea, China) and largely insulated from the Hormuz chokepoint -- though underlying crude feedstock constraints at regional refineries remain a second-order risk.
thailand_gasoline: Thailand's gasoline imports nearly tripled from ~5 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~16 kbd (23-29 Mar), with Malaysia joining Singapore as a second supplier mid-March -- consistent with regional refinery product rebalancing as crude feedstock tightens across Southeast Asia.

All supply comes from Singapore and Malaysia with zero Gulf exposure, but the upward volume trend suggests domestic refining shortfalls are beginning to bite as Thai refiners face higher crude input costs from the crisis.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand's naphtha imports collapsed from 37 kbd (9-15 Mar, 100% Bahrain-sourced) to effectively zero by late March after Bapco's Sitra refinery was destroyed by missile strikes and declared FM on all operations.

A tiny Singapore-origin cargo (~3 kbd, 23-29 Mar) provided marginal relief, but the near-total loss of naphtha feedstock mirrors the crisis hitting FPCC in Taiwan and YNCC in South Korea -- Thai petrochemical crackers face acute feedstock stress if alternative naphtha supply from non-Gulf sources isn't secured rapidly.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam's crude imports collapsed from ~286 kbd (9-15 Mar, 100% Kuwait) to zero by 23-29 Mar -- the most complete supply wipeout among Asian importers, reflecting Vietnam's extreme ~80% pre-crisis dependence on a single Gulf supplier.

A single Nigerian cargo (~88 kbd, 16-22 Mar) briefly filled the gap before flows ceased entirely, exposing the Nghi Son Refinery's near-total reliance on Kuwaiti crude that PM Chinh's direct call to Kuwait's PM has so far failed to restore.

Hanoi's emergency measures -- Decree 72/2026 cutting fuel import tariffs to 0% and securing 4 million additional barrels from international partners -- buy weeks, not months; without Hormuz reopening or a structural pivot to US/Russian crude, Vietnam's refinery throughput will continue declining.
vietnam_kero_jet: Vietnam's jet fuel imports remain entirely sourced from China (~14-20 kbd) and Singapore (~13 kbd), with zero Gulf exposure -- a structural feature of its regional product supply chain.

Volumes are modest but stable at 17-27 kbd through March, suggesting Vietnam's aviation fuel needs are being met through intra-Asian product trade even as crude feedstock for its own refineries has dried up.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Vietnam's diesel imports nearly tripled from ~77 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~218 kbd (23-29 Mar), with South Korea supplying 72-100% of volumes -- a direct consequence of Nghi Son Refinery running at reduced capacity after Kuwait crude supply ceased.
Taiwan appeared as a new diesel supplier in 23-29 Mar (~48 kbd), likely reflecting FPCC's export of refined products as it draws down crude inventories while its crackers run at reduced rates.

The rising import trend signals growing domestic product shortfalls; India also appeared as a supplier in 30 Mar-2 Apr (~40 kbd), suggesting Vietnam is casting a wider net as regional refinery capacity tightens across Asia.
vietnam_gasoline: Vietnam's gasoline imports dropped from ~43 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~17-23 kbd through mid-to-late March before recovering to ~30 kbd in early April -- virtually 100% sourced from Singapore throughout, with Japan providing one early cargo.

The steady Singapore dependence is manageable for now, but Singapore's own refining margins are under pressure from tighter Gulf-origin crude and naphtha feedstock, which could constrain availability if the crisis extends past April.
vietnam_naphtha: Vietnam received zero naphtha imports for three consecutive weeks (9 Mar-29 Mar) before a large Russian cargo appeared at ~85 kbd in 30 Mar-2 Apr -- a significant structural shift and likely the first tangible result of Hanoi's discussions with Moscow on bilateral energy trade.

This Russian naphtha arrival aligns with the broader trend of Moscow backfilling Gulf-linked supply gaps across Asia; if sustained, it would represent a fundamental reorientation of Vietnam's petrochemical feedstock supply chain away from Gulf dependence.
united_states_crude: US crude imports declined from ~3.1 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~2.4 mb/d (23-29 Mar) -- a 22% contraction -- but Gulf-origin barrels (Saudi + Iraq) have proved surprisingly resilient at 500-710 kbd daily, suggesting some Aramco cargoes are still transiting via Red Sea/Suez or were loaded pre-closure.

The Western Hemisphere supply fortress held firm: Venezuela (~473-493 kbd), Mexico (~252-280 kbd), Canada (~173-481 kbd), and Guyana/Brazil provided the backbone, while new suppliers appeared -- Ghana (77 kbd, 23-29 Mar) and Ecuador -- reflecting US refiners actively broadening their Atlantic Basin crude slate.

Saudi Arabia remained the #1 or #2 supplier in 3 of 4 weeks, averaging ~480 kbd -- likely reflecting Yanbu pipeline-routed cargoes bypassing Hormuz, consistent with Aramco's East-West Pipeline running at maximum 7 mb/d capacity.

With Brent at $108-112 and US gas past $4/gal per CNBC, the relatively modest ~8% US dependence on Persian Gulf crude is limiting the direct physical impact, but the global price surge transmits the pain regardless of import source.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports are almost entirely sourced from South Korea (60-162 kbd daily across March), with Canada providing a steady ~24-35 kbd supplement -- zero Gulf exposure reflects the Pacific Basin product trade rebalancing as Korean refiners export surplus products while competing for non-Gulf crude.

China disappeared as a jet fuel supplier after providing ~44 kbd in early March, consistent with Beijing's fuel export ban imposed Mar 4-5 to protect domestic supply -- Taiwan briefly appeared (~10 kbd, 23-29 Mar) filling part of that gap.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports are dominated by Canada at 89-151 kbd daily with zero Gulf exposure, showing the North American pipeline-linked supply chain is insulated from Hormuz disruptions.

Turkey provided a notable ~64 kbd cargo in early March (9-15 Mar) but disappeared thereafter; South Korea emerged as a small but growing supplier (~5-21 kbd) from late March, reflecting Asian refinery product rebalancing toward the US market.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports held at 289-371 kbd through March before surging to ~545 kbd in 30 Mar-2 Apr, with the supplier mix broadening to include India (~44-49 kbd) and South Korea (~25 kbd) alongside traditional Atlantic Basin sources -- a direct response to domestic refinery margins being squeezed by $108+ Brent crude.

Canada remains the anchor supplier at ~98-202 kbd, but the appearance of Indian gasoline is notable: MRPL declared FM on gasoline exports in early March, yet other Indian refiners are clearly finding it profitable to ship product to the US at current crack spreads.

The Bahamas and US Virgin Islands provide consistent transshipment volumes (~42-107 kbd combined), while UK gasoline arrivals have been volatile (5-120 kbd) -- the overall trend is toward a more diversified but higher-cost gasoline import slate.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha imports shifted dramatically from European sources (Belgium, Netherlands, France at ~67 kbd combined, 9-15 Mar) to South American/African origins (Angola, Brazil, Argentina at ~96 kbd, 23-29 Mar), suggesting European crackers are retaining naphtha domestically as Gulf feedstock tightens.

LyondellBasell's FM on European polyolefins due to naphtha cost surges of $250-300/mt explains the European supply withdrawal -- with crackers operating at reduced rates to avoid losses, surplus naphtha for US export has evaporated.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan's crude imports collapsed 66% from ~864 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~294 kbd (23-29 Mar) as Kuwait and Saudi Arabia disappeared entirely from the supplier mix, leaving the US as the sole source -- a precarious single-supplier dependency replacing the pre-crisis Gulf dependency.

Gulf share plunged from 66% (Kuwait + Saudi at ~286 kbd each, 9-15 Mar) to 0% by 23-29 Mar, the most rapid supplier wipeout among major Asian importers; Taiwan's ~150 days of oil reserves per the Atlantic Council provide a buffer, but summer electricity demand could surge 40% above February levels.

The US is now supplying 100% of Taiwan's crude at ~294-524 kbd -- a remarkable strategic shift that deepens Taiwan's energy dependence on Washington, with implications for cross-strait dynamics and semiconductor supply chain continuity per Atlantic Council analysis.

FPCC's crackers at ~50-70% capacity with naphtha stocks depleted create a cascading risk: if crude arrivals slow further, Taiwan faces not just a fuel shortage but a petrochemical crisis that disrupts global PVC and polyethylene supply chains.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan's LNG imports held at 61-76 ktons/d but underwent a radical source rotation: Qatar dropped from 38% share (26 ktons/d, 9-15 Mar) to zero by late March as QatarEnergy's FM extended to all Asian buyers, with Australia stepping up to 41-57 ktons/d to fill the gap.

With only 11 days of natural gas inventory -- far below Japan and South Korea's reserves -- Taiwan is the most LNG-vulnerable economy in East Asia; the appearance of Equatorial Guinea (~10 ktons/d, 23-29 Mar) and US LNG (~18 ktons/d, 30 Mar-2 Apr) shows emergency procurement is active but volumes are modest vs. lost Qatari supply.

Oman maintained a steady ~10 ktons/d contribution from its non-Hormuz 10.4 Mtpa facility, but summer power demand could push Taiwan's gas needs 40% higher than current levels -- if Qatar LNG remains offline through mid-June per QatarEnergy's FM timeline, power rationing and semiconductor fabrication disruptions become a real risk.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan's diesel imports fell from ~97 kbd (9-15 Mar, South Korea) to just ~18 kbd (23-29 Mar) before recovering to ~48 kbd in early April as Russia emerged as the dominant supplier (~37 kbd) alongside China (~10 kbd).

The Russia pivot for diesel is notable: with South Korea's own refiners under naphtha pressure and fuel exports constrained by their own supply crunch, Taiwan is quietly turning to Russian product -- a politically sensitive but supply-chain-rational response to the crisis.
taiwan_gasoline: Taiwan's gasoline imports are negligible -- zero in most weeks with a single Japanese cargo (~25 kbd, 16-22 Mar) -- reflecting FPCC's domestic refining capacity meeting the island's relatively small gasoline demand even at reduced operating rates.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan's naphtha imports -- the lifeblood of FPCC's crackers -- collapsed from 147 kbd (100% Gulf-sourced: UAE 128 kbd + Iraq 20 kbd, 9-15 Mar) to zero by 23-29 Mar, directly triggering Formosa Plastics Corp's April 1 FM on PVC, HDPE, and polyethylene.

Iraq's naphtha disappeared after Baghdad's FM on all foreign-operated oilfields (Mar 20), while UAE naphtha persisted one additional week (~95 kbd, 16-22 Mar via ADCOP pipeline route) before also ceasing -- the complete loss of both Gulf naphtha sources leaves FPCC's #2 and #3 crackers facing potential full shutdown.

With no replacement naphtha cargoes arriving and naphtha prices past $1,000/mt per Formosa reporting, Taiwan's petrochemical output is effectively halving -- cascading through global construction (PVC) and packaging (HDPE) supply chains, exactly the downstream crisis the Atlantic Council warned about.
